title: Lime

overview.description: A small, aromatic citrus fruit with intensely sour juice and a fragrant green peel.

method: Cut it in half or into wedges.
Squeeze the juice over food or into a drink.
The green peel can be finely grated to use as zest.
A whole lime is usually too sour to eat like an apple, although the flesh is edible.

learning.did-you-know: Limes are often picked while they are still green, whereas many varieties of lemon are allowed to ripen until yellow. Some limes will eventually turn yellow as they mature.
learning.what-you-will-learn: How limes compare with lemons and how acidity and aroma can vary between different citrus fruits.

notes.why-its-in-the-chapman-kitchen: A versatile citrus fruit that provides a useful comparison with lemon and introduces children to the way different fruits can be used to add acidity and aroma to food.
chapman-tips: Limes are usually smaller than lemons.
The peel is commonly green, although some limes turn yellow as they ripen.
Lime juice is strongly acidic and can make food taste brighter.
The zest contains fragrant oils and has a different aroma from lemon zest.
Limes are especially common in many Southeast Asian, Mexican, Caribbean and South Asian dishes.
The juice and zest are both useful in cooking.
Limes are often juicier when they feel slightly heavy for their size and have a little give when gently squeezed.
notes.leftovers: Lime juice can be frozen in ice cube trays. Zest can be frozen separately for later use.
